# Performance

<!-- AUTO-INDEX:START (generated by `python -m engram topics` — do not edit by hand) -->
## Atom index (41 atoms, auto-generated from atoms.jsonl)

_Every atom under this topic with its source proof link. Regenerate with `python -m engram topics`._

### claim (12)
- **Adaptability means writing code to enable change: reusable extensible components, beautifully crafted interfaces, and configurability.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **With the right designs, adaptability can increase velocity by reducing the size of the changes needed to add new features.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Adding adaptability naturally adds abstraction and indirection, which usually hurts performance — but the trade-off is usually worth it for the adaptability gained.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Where to sit on the triangle depends on the stage of your project, not a fixed 'always aim for the middle' rule.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Performance is a problem, but it's not usually the first problem — be deliberate about which way you're leaning.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Premature optimization usually occurs for micro performance — typically the code-review comment that you should do X instead of Y because X is faster.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Some argue functions are expensive, but rarely is the cost of a function so significant that removing it is the solution to a performance problem — and even then it doesn't prove the readability loss is worth it.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **A big issue with making performance rules to follow is that the rules often have a lot of exceptions.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Measuring is critical because your assumptions about what will make things faster can actually make them slower.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Data-structure selection is by far the most important factor, because the right data structure gives dramatically better results than the wrong one.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Sometimes when things are slow it's just one silly thing slowing everything down.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **A lot of performance comes from how code uses memory: allocating memory (a new object or array) slows critical sections because the system must find free chunks, and keeping critical elements close in memory exploits the CPU cache.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`

### concept (1)
- **Performance is one element of a trade-off triangle whose other two corners are velocity and adaptability.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`

### definition (3)
- **Velocity is how quickly one adds new features.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Adaptability is how well the system can change to new requirements.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Macro performance is design-level, system-wide performance; micro performance is fine-tuned performance of a single module or function.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`

### method (4)
- **There's only one way to properly optimize: measure, try something, measure again.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Think in '80% moves' first — changes that could give an 80% reduction — and often only data-structure changes get you that far.** (80% reduction) — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **If 80% moves aren't enough, use a profiler to find the hot spots — the functions that are most expensive.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **The ordered optimization recipe: have a real performance problem, measure it, make 80% moves by swapping data structures or moving to a faster algorithm, profile for hot spots, and worst case start reasoning about what the code does under the hood.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`

### result (4)
- **For an int, pre-increment and post-increment disassemble to identical code (load into a register, add one, store back).** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **For a vector iterator, post-increment has four extra assembly lines to copy the iterator and call into the other operator.** (4 extra assembly lines) — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **But once optimization is turned on, those extra function calls completely disappear and you get identical code for both ++i and i++.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Measuring shows the set is actually slower when only a few users are logged in (the normal case), likely because the array's integers sit adjacent in memory while the set allocates objects scattered around, reducing cache hits.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`

### number (1)
- **Measured on his MacBook, the slowest increment is 3.4 nanoseconds.** (3.4 nanoseconds) — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`

### example (6)
- **A feature-complete game near its ship date would focus on performance, accepting reduced velocity and adaptability; a game early in development would favor features and an extensible system.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Zuckerberg wrote Facebook in PHP — an awkward language that caused scaling issues; they later built a PHP-to-C++ compiler and then a PHP dialect called Hack.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **His own animation Python code linear-searches a big character array every frame and still renders the videos fast enough that it's not worth making faster.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **C++ has ++i and i++; proponents claim ++i is faster because i++ must copy the value, since when part of a larger expression the increment must happen after evaluation.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Example: a logged-in-users class searches an array with a for loop; a reviewer says to use a set for faster unique lookups.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **A player named 'tossed' profiled GTA 5 Online without source code, found nearly all the time was spent parsing a JSON file, and a patch fixing just that improved load time by 70%.** (70% load-time improvement) — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`

### caveat (1)
- **Don't worry about memory-level concerns until you know you have a performance problem and have tried other things first.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`

### opinion (6)
- **The creator truly believes that premature optimization is the root of all evil.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Most conversations about performance are a total waste of time — not because performance isn't important, but because people feel very, very strongly about it.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Writing Facebook in inconsistent, inefficient PHP was the right move because it let Zuckerberg build quickly; once performance became a real problem, engineers solved it — starting in optimized C might have meant it never took off.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **You shouldn't blindly trust a claim that something is faster; verify it — which here meant disassembling the code.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Ask whether the conversation is even worth it: if figuring out which is technically faster brings you joy, go for it, but it shouldn't end up in a code review because it doesn't matter.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[CORE]`
- **Until you've shown a function is the leading cause of performance issues, go with what's more readable; here the set is cleaner and less bug-prone, so he'd still choose it.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`

### warning (2)
- **Pure velocity — hacking something together by the shortest path, future maintainers be damned — gives immediate speed but creates technical debt that eventually weighs you to a halt.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
- **Too much adaptability also hurts velocity: building for a bunch of use cases that never happen just ends up being wasted time.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`

### style (1)
- **He likes i++ because he thinks it looks prettier and sticks with it until someone measures that the app is too slow and that changing it is the fix.** — [tKbV6BpH-C8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tKbV6BpH-C8) `[STATED]`
